museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: July 2019: Fire Emblem: Three Houses becomes the Switch summer obsession.
museum.curatorNote: Initial researched draft for July 2019. Recent enough for careful source review before verification.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight notable releases, led by month-specific anchors where evidence supports them.

items.title: Fire Emblem: Three Houses
items.body: Nintendo turns tactical school life into a summer-long obsession.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for July 26, 2019.

items.title: Resident Evil 2
items.body: Capcom remakes a classic into something modern, heavy and precise.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for January 25, 2019.

items.title: Kingdom Hearts III
items.body: A long-promised sequel finally arrives carrying years of fandom weight.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for January 29, 2019.

items.title: Apex Legends
items.body: Respawn shadow-drops a battle royale and briefly changes the temperature of every party chat.
items.sourceNote: Widely reported as a February 4, 2019 launch.

items.title: Devil May Cry 5
items.body: Capcom's action lineage returns with style loud enough to fill the room.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for March 8, 2019.

items.title: Sekiro: Shadows Die Twice
items.body: FromSoftware changes the rhythm from endurance to deflection.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for March 22, 2019.

items.title: Days Gone
items.body: Sony Bend's biker apocalypse becomes one of PS4's more divisive exclusives.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for April 26, 2019.

items.title: Control
items.body: Remedy makes concrete architecture, office myth and telekinesis feel stylishly haunted.
items.sourceNote: Widely listed for August 27, 2019.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Platform, buying and industry context for the month and its wider year.

items.title: Nintendo Switch Lite launches
items.body: Nintendo makes Switch cheaper, smaller and more handheld, reinforcing the console's dual identity.
items.sourceNote: 2019 hardware references list September 2019 Switch Lite launch.

items.title: Google Stadia launches
items.body: Cloud gaming arrives as a consumer service with more curiosity than confidence.
items.sourceNote: Google/Gematsu/GameSpot sources list November 19, 2019.

items.title: Apple Arcade launches
items.body: Mobile games get a curated subscription pitch without ads or in-app purchases.
items.sourceNote: Apple confirms September 19, 2019.

items.title: Next-gen consoles become named futures
items.body: PlayStation 5 and Xbox Project Scarlett move from rumour to holiday-2020 expectation.
items.sourceNote: Axios and E3 2019 reporting support the next-gen framing.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: How the network felt around the edges of play.

items.title: Streams and servers
items.body: Apex Legends proves a game can appear from nowhere and own the week through streams, clips and squad chat.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period reporting and UK access patterns; verify with period web/magazine sources before final publication.

items.title: Services and stores
items.body: Stadia and Apple Arcade make the word platform feel less like a box and more like a service plan.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period reporting and UK access patterns; verify with period web/magazine sources before final publication.

items.title: Forums and feeds
items.body: Epic Store exclusives keep PC forums busy, with storefront choice becoming part of release-day emotion.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period reporting and UK access patterns; verify with period web/magazine sources before final publication.

items.title: Everyday connection
items.body: Discord, Twitch, ResetEra, Reddit and YouTube shape the first draft of a game's reputation before many people have played it.
items.sourceNote: Online-life context is synthesised from platform history, period reporting and UK access patterns; verify with period web/magazine sources before final publication.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A curator's narrative sketch of the month as lived culture.

items.title: The room
items.body: It felt like a waiting room with excellent magazines on the table: the next generation was coming, but the current machines still had things to say.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.

items.title: The shelf
items.body: The year's best games were odd shapes: a detective RPG about failure, a delivery epic about connection, a battle royale dropped without warning.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.

items.title: The conversation
items.body: Services kept knocking on the glass. Stadia, Apple Arcade and Game Pass all suggested that ownership might become less central.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.

items.title: The afterimage
items.body: In the UK, the old rituals still held: boxed charts, Black Friday bundles, train-ride Switch sessions and YouTube tabs open beside the telly.
items.sourceNote: Interpretive narrative based on researched anchors; keep labelled as atmosphere rather than fact.
